records.summary: Page 1 of the RT-DETR research paper manuscript containing the conclusion of the Introduction, a list of main contributions, and the beginning of the Related Work section. The text details RT-DETR performance metrics compared to YOLO and DETR models. The page follows a two-column academic format and ends with a sentence fragment continuing to page 2.
records.analysis:
The image displays page 1 of a research paper titled 'DETRs Beat YOLOs on Real-time Object Detection', formatted in a standard two-column academic layout with black text on a white background. The page content begins mid-sentence with the phrase 'computational redundancy and further improve accuracy,' continuing from the Introduction section on the previous page. The upper portion of the page completes the Introduction, discussing the redesign of the encoder for RT-DETR, the uncertainty-minimal query selection, and flexible speed tuning. Specific performance metrics appear in the text, stating RT-DETR-R50 achieves 53.1% AP on COCO and 108 FPS on T4 GPU, with a reference to Figure 1 located nearby in the text flow. 

Below the Introduction conclusion, a paragraph outlines the main contributions summarized as points (i), (ii), and (iii), detailing the proposal of RT-DETR, the analysis of NMS impact, and support for flexible speed tuning. The lower half of the page introduces Section 2, 'Related Work,' with bolded section headers. Subsection 2.1, 'Real-time Object Detectors,' discusses YOLOv1 and CNN-based architectures. Subsection 2.2, 'End-to-end Object Detectors,' covers DETR, Deformable-DETR, DINO, and other variants. Citations appear as bracketed numbers throughout the text (e.g., [45], [42], [31]). The text at the bottom of the right column cuts off mid-word at 'Optimizing query initial-', indicating continuation onto page 2. The visual style utilizes standard LaTeX formatting with serif fonts for body text and bold sans-serif or serif fonts for headers. No actual figures or tables are visually embedded on this specific page segment, though Figure 1 is referenced in the text.

records.entity_name: RT-DETR Paper Page 2 - NMS Analysis and Speed Benchmark (image)
records.graph_node_id: RT-DETR Paper Page 2 - NMS Analysis and Speed Benchmark (image) [image_asset p1]
records.summary: This image represents page two of the RT-DETR research paper, focusing on the limitations of YOLO detectors due to Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS). The page features Figure 2 illustrating box counts at varying confidence thresholds and Table 1 quantifying the trade-off between NMS hyperparameters, accuracy (AP), and execution time. Section 3 establishes an end-to-end speed benchmark to justify the need for RT-DETR. The content bridges related work on DETRs with the empirical analysis motivating the proposed real-time detector.
records.analysis: The image displays page two of the research paper 'DETRs Beat YOLOs on Real-time Object Detection'. The layout follows a standard two-column academic format with black text on a white background. The top section concludes Section 2.2 'End-to-end Object Detectors', discussing query initialization methods like Conditional DETR and DINO. Below the text sits Figure 2, captioned 'The number of boxes at different confidence thresholds', depicted as a bar graph illustrating NMS sensitivity to hyperparameters. The main body introduces Section 3 'End-to-end Speed of Detectors', specifically subsection 3.1 'Analysis of NMS'. Section 3.1 details the impact of Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) hyperparameters on YOLOv5 and YOLOv8. A central element is Table 1, titled 'The effect of IoU threshold and confidence threshold on accuracy and NMS execution time'. Table 1 presents data comparing IoU thresholds (0.5, 0.6, 0.8) and Confidence thresholds (0.001, 0.01, 0.05) against Average Precision (AP) and NMS execution time in milliseconds. The bottom section begins subsection 3.2 'End-to-end Speed Benchmark', outlining the methodology for fair speed comparison. The visual style is formal and technical, consistent with computer vision conference proceedings.

records.graph_node_id: Kongzi Paper Page 1 with Figure 1 (image) [image_asset p0]
records.summary: This image is the first page of the research paper introducing 'Kongzi,' a large language model optimized for historical accuracy. It features the abstract and introduction text alongside Figure 1, which illustrates the model's capability to detect and correct factual hallucinations in historical narratives compared to other LLMs. The figure uses a comparative layout to show how misleading prompts can lead to errors and how Kongzi addresses this through structured fact-checking.
records.analysis:
The image displays the first page of an academic paper titled 'Kongzi: A Historical Large Language Model with Fact Enhancement,' marked as an 'Anonymous submission.' The layout is a standard two-column format on a white background with black serif text. 

On the left column, the 'Abstract' and 'Introduction' sections are visible. The Abstract outlines the proposal of 'Kongzi,' a large language model designed to address factual inaccuracies in historical reasoning by integrating historical corpora and a fact-enhanced reinforcement learning strategy. The Introduction elaborates on the limitations of existing models regarding hallucinations in historical contexts and details the methodology: collecting domain-specific corpora, performing continued pre-training, instruction-following fine-tuning, and introducing a novel reward function based on entity-relation graphs.

On the right side, there is a prominent diagram labeled 'Figure 1: Comparative analysis of different LLM's responses to misleading historical fact-checking queries.' This figure visually demonstrates the problem Kongzi aims to solve. It contains three stacked rectangular panels showing different AI model responses to a query about the Battle of Changban and the character Xing Daorong. 
- The top panel shows a response containing a factual error (hallucination) regarding Xing Daorong's role, accompanied by a small icon of a person.
- The middle panel shows a response correctly identifying Xing Daorong as a fictional character from 'Romance of the Three Kingdoms,' marked with a blue checkmark icon.
- The bottom panel highlights specific factual discrepancies in a table format, comparing 'Official History' vs. 'In Three Kingdoms,' with red 'X' marks indicating errors in the model's output regarding events like 'Cao Cao beheaded Xing Daorong.'

To the right of these text panels is a schematic diagram illustrating the '5 Ws' of fact-checking (Who, What, Where, When, Why) arranged in a star shape around a central node labeled 'Event/Fact.' Red arrows point from the text to this diagram, and a caption below reads, 'Misleading prompts may affect an LLM's perception of facts.' The visual style is clean and academic, utilizing red and blue accents in the figure to distinguish between correct and incorrect information.

records.summary: This image captures a page from the 'Kongzi' research paper, detailing the methodology for creating a historical large language model. It outlines the model's core contributions—continued pre-training, CoT data generation, and fact-aware reinforcement learning—and provides specific technical details on data acquisition, including the processing of 0.2 billion tokens of Chinese historical text. The content bridges the gap between related work in reasoning models and the specific implementation strategies used to enhance factual accuracy in historical domains.
records.analysis:
The image displays a single page from an academic research paper titled 'Kongzi: A Historical Large Language Model with Fact Enhancement.' The layout is a standard two-column format typical of conference proceedings, set against a white background with black serif typography. 

The left column begins with a summary of contributions, listing three bullet points: 'Continued Pre-training,' 'CoT Data Generation,' and 'Fact-Aware RL.' Below this is the 'Related Work' section, subdivided into 'Reasoning in Large Language Models' (discussing prompt engineering, Chain-of-Thought fine-tuning, and Reinforcement Learning) and 'Chinese History in Large Language Models' (referencing datasets like FoodieQA and benchmarks like CTM). The text cites various authors such as Plaat et al., DeepSeek-AI, and Wang et al.

The right column continues the discussion on Chinese history LLMs before transitioning to the 'Method' section. This section outlines the pipeline: data collection, incremental pre-training, cold-start instruction fine-tuning, and fact-enhanced reinforcement learning. Under the subsection 'Data acquisition and processing,' the text details the selection of ancient Chinese history texts (0.2 billion tokens) and describes a classification workflow where historical corpora are separated into 'factual statements' and 'contextual reasoning texts' using a small-parameter LLM-assisted process. The text explains that sentences are segmented based on punctuation and assigned unique identifiers to maintain semantic coherence.

records.entity_name: Machine Learning Pipeline Overview and Training Strategy Page (image)
records.graph_node_id: Machine Learning Pipeline Overview and Training Strategy Page (image) [image_asset p2]
records.summary: This image presents Figure 2 and accompanying text from a research paper describing a multi-stage machine learning pipeline. The workflow involves data cleaning, augmentation, and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) generation, followed by Continue Pre-Training (CPT), two-stage Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), and Content Reinforcement Learning (RL). The text elaborates on data generation strategies, initial training to enhance reasoning capabilities, and a reinforcement learning approach using a Historical Fact-enhanced Reward Module (HFRM) to reduce hallucinations.
records.analysis: The image displays a page from a technical document, likely a research paper, detailing a machine learning pipeline. The top half features 'Figure 2: Overview of our pipeline,' a flowchart diagram. The process begins on the left with an 'Original Corpus' icon feeding into a 'Data Cleaning' module (light blue). This splits into 'Factual Data' and 'Inference Text' (yellow boxes). Factual data goes through 'Data Augmentation' to a 'CPT' (Continue Pre-Training) node (orange oval with fire icon). Inference text undergoes 'Chunking' and 'Generate' steps to create 'CoT Data' (Chain-of-Thought), which feeds into the first 'SFT' (Supervised Fine-Tuning) node. The output of CPT and CoT Data are concatenated (circle with plus sign) before entering the SFT stage. There are two sequential SFT nodes, followed by a final 'Content RL' (Reinforcement Learning) node (green box). A legend below defines symbols for Operation Flow, Concatenation, Training, SFT Process, CPT Process, and Functional Module. Below the figure is a caption explaining the data processing and training stages. The bottom half contains text sections titled '(2) Generation of Primary SFT Data', '(3) Generation of Secondary SFT Data', 'Initial training strategy for the model', and 'Reinforcement Learning Training'. It includes mathematical equations labeled (1) and (2) defining variables X and M(r), along with a bulleted list defining terms like G, o_i, r_ratio, epsilon, and A_hat_i,t.

records.entity_name: HFRM Architecture and GRPO Objective Function Diagram (image)
records.graph_node_id: HFRM Architecture and GRPO Objective Function Diagram (image) [image_asset p3]
records.summary: This image presents Figure 3 from a technical paper, illustrating the Historical Fact-enhanced Reward Module (HFRM). The diagram visualizes a pipeline where a Policy Model generates text that is evaluated against Ground Truth using knowledge graph embeddings and cosine similarity. The accompanying text defines the mathematical framework for Generalized Reward Policy Optimization (GRPO), emphasizing the use of semantic similarity as a reward signal to improve factual accuracy in AI models.
records.analysis: The image displays a technical diagram and accompanying text from a research paper, detailing the 'Historical Fact-enhanced Reward Module (HFRM)'. The top section features a flowchart illustrating a reinforcement learning process. It begins with an input 'q' feeding into a yellow 'Policy Model' box marked with a fire icon. This model generates a sequence of outputs ($o_1$ to $o_G$). These outputs are processed by two blue boxes: a 'Reference Model' and a 'Reward Model', both marked with snowflake icons. The Reward Model produces reward values ($r_1$ to $r_G$), which undergo 'Group Computation' to yield final actions or scores ($A_1$ to $A_G$). Below this main flow is a detailed sub-diagram enclosed in a red dashed rectangle, explaining the internal mechanism of the Reward Model. This sub-process shows parallel paths for 'Ground Truth' and 'Generation'. Both paths involve 'Information Extraction' (represented by vertical lists of squares), conversion into knowledge graphs (nodes connected by lines), and 'Embedding' (blue vertical bars with snowflakes). The embeddings from both paths are compared using 'Cosine Similarity' to determine semantic alignment. The bottom half of the image contains dense academic text defining variables like $G$, $o_i$, and $\varepsilon_{KL}$, presenting the objective function for Generalized Reward Policy Optimization (GRPO), and explaining how the HFRM uses factuality scores to mitigate hallucinations in long-form text generation.

records.entity_name: Kongzi Model Performance Chart and Experimental Methodology Page (image)
records.graph_node_id: Kongzi Model Performance Chart and Experimental Methodology Page (image) [image_asset p4]
records.summary: This image shows Figure 4 comparing Kongzi model variants against other LLMs under three judge models, alongside detailed experimental methodology including dataset curation, preprocessing, and multi-stage training protocols using classical Chinese historical corpora and GPU infrastructure.
records.analysis: The image displays page 5 of a technical document, featuring a bar chart at the top and two columns of text below. The chart, titled 'Figure 4: Comparison of Kongzi’s scores with other models under different judges,' presents performance data for eight AI models across three evaluation categories: GPT-4o, Gemini-2.0-flash-lite-preview, and Qwen-Max. Each category contains a group of vertical bars representing model scores, color-coded according to a legend on the right: orange for Kongzi-0.5B, light yellow for Kongzi-7B, and various shades of blue for Deepseek-r1-distill variants (7B, 14B, 32B), Deepseek-r1, O3-mini, and QwQ-32B-preview. Numerical values are displayed atop each bar. Below the chart, the caption explains that the score is a weighted sum of response process (0.8), thought process (0.1), and outperformance ratio against Deepseek-r1 sample (0.1). The lower half contains structured sections: 'Experiments' with subsections 'Datasets and Evaluation Protocols' and 'Data Processing & Generating' in the left column; and 'Base Model Training', 'Continued PreTraining', and 'Supervised Fine-Tuning' in the right column. Text details dataset construction from 200 million tokens of Chinese historical texts, categorization into factual statements and contextual reasoning, preprocessing via LLM-generated QA pairs, and training procedures using NVIDIA GPUs (A6000 and RTX 3090) over specified durations. All text is black on white background, formatted in standard academic style with bold headings and numbered lists.

records.entity_name: Kongzi Model Performance Metrics and RL Framework Documentation (image)
records.graph_node_id: Kongzi Model Performance Metrics and RL Framework Documentation (image) [image_asset p5]
records.summary: This image contains a page from a research paper detailing the performance improvements of the 'Kongzi' AI models (0.5B and 7B parameters) achieved through Reinforcement Learning (RL) training. It features a comprehensive data table comparing RL-trained models against SFT baselines across metrics like logical reasoning and problem solving when evaluated against GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5, and Qwen-Max. The text further elaborates on the GRPO reinforcement learning framework, defining specific reward functions for factual accuracy, formatting, and repetition penalties used to optimize the model's output.
records.analysis: The image displays page 6 of a technical document, featuring a structured layout with black text on a white background. The top half is dominated by 'Table 1', titled 'Improvement of Kongzi model performance in various metrics by RL training.' This table compares the performance of 'Kongzi-0.5B' and 'Kongzi-7B' models against three base models: 'GPT-4o', 'Gemini-2.5', and 'Qwen-Max'. The columns list specific metrics: 'Think Score', 'Answer Score', 'Historical Accuracy', 'Logical Reasoning', 'Problem Solving', and '>Deepseek-r1 Ratio'. Rows are divided into 'SFT' (Supervised Fine-Tuning) and 'RL' (Reinforcement Learning) variants. Bold text highlights the superior scores of the RL-trained models, with parenthetical values indicating the percentage improvement over the SFT versions. Below the table, the text is organized into two columns detailing the methodology. The left column describes the training stages, including 'block-level random mixing' and fine-tuning on 'Chain-of-Thought (CoT)' examples. It introduces the 'Reinforcement Learning Training' section, specifying the use of the 'GRPO' framework and a reward function based on cosine similarity between knowledge graphs, represented by the formula ER = cos(f(G_model), f(G_gold)). The right column defines G_model and G_gold, then lists specific reward components: 'Format Reward' (checking for markers like <|begin_of_thought|>) and 'Repetition Penalty Reward' (penalizing repeated n-grams using the formula RP = RR x MPV). Finally, it presents the 'Integration of Reward Functions' with a weighted aggregation formula: R_final = w1*R_cos + w2*R_log + w3*R_fmt + w4*R_rep.

records.entity_name: Kongzi Model Performance Evaluation and Conclusion Page (image)
records.graph_node_id: Kongzi Model Performance Evaluation and Conclusion Page (image) [image_asset p6]
records.summary: This image captures page 7 of a research paper introducing 'Kongzi,' a language model designed for historical domains. It features Table 2, presenting benchmark results on CMMLU-Chinese-History comparing Kongzi against models like Qwen and Deepseek. The text details the evaluation methodology involving human and LLM scoring and concludes with a summary of the model's achievements through reinforcement learning and cold-start training, followed by a list of references.
records.analysis: The image displays page 7 of a technical research paper, formatted in a standard two-column academic layout with black text on a white background. The left column is dominated by 'Table 2: Results on CMMLU-Chinese-History,' which is subdivided into three sections: 'Base Model Table,' 'Mixed Reasoning Model Table,' and 'Reasoning Model Table.' These tables compare the performance (accuracy percentages) of various Large Language Models (LLMs), including Qwen, Deepseek r1, and Kongzi variants, across categories like 'Dense Model' and 'Reasoning.' Specific metrics such as 'w/o' (without enhancement prompts), 'Cot' (Chain of Thought), and 'Thinking' are listed. Below the table, a caption explains that bold scores indicate the best results and defines abbreviations. Further down the left column is the 'Results Evaluation' section, detailing the evaluation methodology using manual expert scoring and LLM scoring based on five criteria: Think Score, Answer Score, Historical Accuracy, Logical Reasoning, and Problem Solving. The right column contains the continuation of the results discussion, followed by the 'Conclusion' section, which summarizes the proposal of the 'Kongzi' language model for the historical domain and its effectiveness via reinforcement learning. The page concludes with a 'References' section listing numerous citations in alphabetical order.

records.analysis: The image displays a high-resolution scan of page 8 from an academic document, specifically the bibliography or references section. The layout consists of two columns of dense text on a white background with black serif typography. The content comprises a comprehensive list of citations related to Large Language Models (LLMs), artificial intelligence, and natural language processing. Each entry follows a standard academic citation format, listing authors, publication year, title, and source (e.g., arXiv preprint numbers, conference proceedings like EMNLP, or journals). Notable entries include 'DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in LLMs via Reinforcement Learning' (2025), 'A Survey on Hallucination in Large Language Models' (2025), 'GPT-4 Technical Report' (2024), and 'Qwen2.5 Technical Report' (2025). The text is justified, creating clean vertical edges for both columns.
